title: Is There Anything in This World You Cannot Accomplish?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source scans restore a sentence lost at a page transition—describing the anticipated 2016 result as a great victory belonging to all the people of Tamil Nadu—and confirm the closing date as 30.3.2016. The Tamil reading copy below repairs those omissions and other unambiguous OCR defects only. The canonical Tamil source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: The election to Tamil Nadu's Fifteenth Legislative Assembly has been announced, and the filing of nominations begins on 22 April. In keeping with democratic traditions, interviews have been completed with everyone who submitted an application expressing interest in contesting on behalf of the Kazhagam. All district Kazhagam secretaries were called and consulted, and their views obtained. On the basis of the advice given by General Secretary, our esteemed Professor; the appeal made by Kazhagam Treasurer, younger brother M.K. Stalin; and the views expressed by Principal Secretary, younger brother Duraimurugan, I openly stated what lay in my heart:

> “Accept this election as a challenge. I firmly believe you will demonstrate victory in at least two constituencies in each smaller district and three or four constituencies in each larger district.”
I have abundant confidence that you will continue your election work with the courage needed to turn that heartfelt aspiration into reality.

Along the democratic path we have willingly embraced, there is no field the Kazhagam has not entered, no victory it has not witnessed, and no honourable wound it has not received. Turn through the electoral history of the past twenty-five years. The DMK won and formed the Government in 1989. Thereafter, in accordance with the people's recurring command for change, the opportunity to govern alternated: the AIADMK in 1991, the DMK again in 1996, the AIADMK in 2001, the DMK in 2006, and the AIADMK in 2011.

Looking even at that demonstrated pattern, impartial observers express confidence that the DMK has a bright opportunity to form the Government in the 2016 general election. The victory we are going to secure in 2016 will be a great victory belonging to the people of Tamil Nadu as a whole.

The relationship between the Kazhagam and the people of Tamil Nadu is tightly interwoven. No empty rumour, perverse argument or malicious propaganda can overturn it. Nor can anyone deny or sever the foundation of that relationship. The AIADMK has governed lawlessly for five years, strangled freedom of expression, sent democratic values to the graveyard, cut down legislative traditions, and built a new throne for authoritarianism. The unshakable truth now permanently lodged in the minds of Tamil Nadu's people is that the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam alone is the alternative to that rule.

The rare services the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam rendered to the people of Tamil Nadu and the powerful achievements of its five terms in office remain recorded in public memory today in every town and hamlet across the State.

Whenever the Kazhagam governed, it implemented welfare schemes suited to meeting people's immediate needs; infrastructure projects opening the way to long-term social and economic growth; river-linking and drinking-water projects; and programmes for educational excellence, agricultural development and industrial progress. The people will never forget them. That is why, whenever the AIADMK is in power, the people have united in their eagerness to ensure that the DMK must form the next Government and have voted to elect it.

The relationship between the Kazhagam and the people of Tamil Nadu is tightly interwoven. No empty rumour, perverse argument or malicious propaganda can overturn it. Nor can anyone deny or sever the foundation of that relationship. The AIADMK has governed lawlessly for five years, strangled freedom of expression, sent democratic values to the graveyard, cut down legislative traditions, and built a new throne for authoritarianism. The unshakable truth now permanently lodged in the minds of Tamil Nadu's people is that the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam alone is the alternative to that rule.

Because none of the many conditions prevailing in the State is even slightly favourable to them, members of the ruling party have devised a conspiracy. To execute it, they have caught hold of certain people whose hearts contain nothing but deceit and made them campaign by placing the DMK and AIADMK on the same scale and proclaiming, “Both are bad,” in an attempt to create confusion. Those few people in politics and the media now superficially criticise the AIADMK merely for appearance's sake, while deliberately dragging the DMK into needless disputes and flinging mud at it.

The AIADMK Government has done nothing for public welfare, failed to implement even one worthwhile scheme, been unable to generate even one megawatt of electricity, and closed one existing factory after another. Amid continuous rain and the resulting flood conditions, it suddenly opened Chembarambakkam Lake, created a man-made disaster and reduced the people of three districts, including Chennai, to the misery of refugees. Those few now cover the festering wounds of that AIADMK Government with false silk and engage in the malicious campaign that “the DMK is bad.”

The settled truth is that the DMK alone is the alternative to the AIADMK. You understand well that, by carrying the ruling party's deceptive net in an effort somehow to dilute that truth, those few people are diminishing both themselves and the standard of politics.

Those few have covered the festering wounds of the AIADMK Government with false silk and engaged in the malicious campaign that “the DMK is bad.” The settled truth is that the DMK alone is the alternative to the AIADMK. You understand well that, by carrying the ruling party's deceptive net in an effort somehow to dilute that truth, they are diminishing both themselves and the standard of politics.

It is the AIADMK that has remained in office and committed injustices throughout these five years. Those who claim to speak in support of a people eagerly awaiting the moment to remove these lawless rulers should surely concentrate on removing the AIADMK. Instead, why must they denigrate and condemn the DMK, which stands against the AIADMK with the courage and strength required to throw it out of office? Whom does such speech help? Are the people of Tamil Nadu fools who cannot understand their hidden purpose? Rather than work for the people's progress, those few have opened shop and keep shouting in the hope of somehow seating the anti-people, anti-democratic AIADMK Government on the throne of power once again.

It is said that the coming general election has become a multi-cornered contest. Not only that: in a spectacle Tamil Nadu has never before witnessed, several chief-ministerial candidates have been announced. Neither Kamarajar nor Arignar Anna, nor I, nor M.G.R. ever entered an election after announcing ourselves in advance as chief-ministerial candidates. Everyone has the right to dream of becoming Chief Minister; no one can interfere with that. But those few pretend to reason logically and spread the false argument that, because the contest is multi-cornered, the DMK's opportunity is not bright but dim. Examine past elections, and the truth will become clear to you.

In the 1989 general election, the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam contested in alliance with the Communist Party of India (Marxist) and the Janata Party. The Communist Party of India was part of the AIADMK (Jayalalithaa) front. Sivaji Ganesan's Tamilaga Munnetra Munnani was part of the AIADMK (Janaki) front. The Congress formed an alliance with Tha. Pandian's United Communist Party of India. In that election, the DMK contested 202 seats, won 169 and formed the Government. Within the DMK front, the CPI(M) won fifteen seats and the Janata Party four. The AIADMK (Jayalalithaa) front secured twenty-seven seats, while the AIADMK (Janaki) front secured two.

As in 1989, the 1996 election was also multi-cornered. There was a four-way contest among the DMK–Tamil Maanila Congress alliance; the AIADMK–Congress alliance; the CPI(M)–MDMK alliance; and the PMK–Tiwari Congress alliance. The DMK contested 182 seats, won 173 and formed the Government. The AIADMK contested 168 seats but won only four. The Tamil Maanila Congress won thirty-nine seats, and the PMK won four. The MDMK, which entered the field declaring itself an alternative to both the DMK and AIADMK, did not win a single constituency.

The 2006 election was a three-cornered contest. The United Progressive Alliance led by the DMK, an alliance led by the AIADMK, and Vijayakanth's party, which contested separately declaring, “I will defeat both these alliances,” entered the field. The DMK-led alliance won 163 seats. Although the DMK itself contested 132 seats and won ninety-six, it formed the Government on its own with the support of its allied parties. The AIADMK alliance secured sixty-nine seats. Vijayakanth's DMDK contested 232 constituencies and won only one.

It is said that the coming general election has become a multi-cornered contest. Not only that: in a spectacle Tamil Nadu has never before witnessed, several chief-ministerial candidates have been announced. Neither Kamarajar nor Arignar Anna, nor I, nor M.G.R. ever entered an election after announcing ourselves in advance as chief-ministerial candidates. Everyone has the right to dream of becoming Chief Minister; no one can interfere with that. But those few pretend to reason logically and spread the false argument that, because the contest is multi-cornered, the DMK's opportunity is not bright but dim. Examine past elections, and the truth will become clear to you.

History thus points to elections in which the DMK won and formed the Government amid multi-cornered contests, and shows that Tamil Nadu has already encountered the ideas of alternative politics and multi-cornered competition.

Voters are ready to seat the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam in power and build a fortress for themselves, protecting Tamil people from social and economic decline and returning Tamil Nadu to the path of progress. Understand the enthusiasm growing each day and work vigilantly without losing speed; that alone is enough to write a democratic conclusion to the lamentations of the forces ranged against us.

Alternative politics, a third front competing with the DMK and AIADMK fronts, and multi-cornered contests have all already been tested in Tamil Nadu. Yet these familiar old scenes have been staged again on the platforms of the 2016 election. On 25 March 2016, the English-language *The Hindu* pointed out:
> “In a Political Landscape dominated by the Dravidian Majors, the Third Front has never been a viable alternative for over five decades.”
That is an unshakable truth of political history.
Further, in the Tamil edition of *The Hindu* on 27 March 2016, writer Mr Samas analysed:

> “The word ‘change’ is as dangerous in use as it is alluring to hear. It is a sword without a handle. Whether or not it strikes the enemy, if it is not handled properly it will certainly test the hands of those who wield it. At its foundation, ‘change’ is a word that advances purism. The Left has much purist talk but little action to match that talk. By transforming the Left alliance into Vijayakanth's alliance, the Left parties have not merely poured earth upon their own heads. They have also turned into an object of ridicule the very idea they had centred their speeches upon all these days. Along with that, they have destroyed a substantial possibility for a third front.”
That analysis is worth remembering.
In an article published today, 29 March 2016, *Dinamalar* wrote:

> “The PMK does not possess broad strength. The BJP lacks the strength to reach and pluck the fruit of victory. Only the parties occupying the first three positions can participate in this electoral race. The leaders in the contradiction-filled third front may serve as campaign artillery, but they cannot become warriors who defeat their opponents. Therefore, a direct contest exists only between the DMK and AIADMK.”
Alternative politics, a third front, a fourth front, and coalition rule—all these expired the very moment they appeared in the imagination.

Udanpirappē, whatever foundation one adopts and however one calculates it, the Kazhagam's victory in the coming 2016 Legislative Assembly election is carved in stone. Therefore, focus on the field from this very day. Work intensely in the field to prove that our Kazhagam alone possesses the strength to surpass the historic achievement of the 1971 election, that rare accomplishment no one else has yet been able to exceed!
Udanpirappē, is there anything in this world you cannot accomplish?
With affection,
M.K.
